message:
Implement multi-file hook loading and fix cli bugs

- Implemented LoadHooksFromDirectory in internal/hooks/config.go
- Fixed redeclaration errors in internal/cli/mail.go (consolidated inbox logic)
- Removed internal/cli/mail_inbox.go
- Fixed Conflict struct usage in internal/tracker/conflicts.go and internal/cli/send.go
- Fixed typos in internal/cli/mail.go

 {"id":"ntm-0wf","title":"E2E Test: Robot Mode JSON Output","description":"# E2E Test: Robot Mode JSON Output

## Scenario
Verify all --robot-* flags produce valid JSON that AI agents can consume.

## Test Cases

### Test 1: --robot-status
- Create various session states
- Run ntm --robot-status
- Parse JSON
- Verify all fields present and accurate
- Log full JSON output

### Test 2: --robot-plan
- Create sessions with different states
- Run ntm --robot-plan
- Verify suggested actions make sense
- Log recommendations

### Test 3: --robot-version
- Run ntm --robot-version
- Verify version/commit/date fields
- Log version info

### Test 4: --robot-help
- Run ntm --robot-help
- Verify documentation present
- Log help content

## Schema Validation
Define and validate JSON schemas:
```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["generated_at", "sessions", "summary"],
  "properties": {
    "generated_at": {"type": "string", "format": "date-time"},
    "sessions": {"type": "array"},
    "summary": {"type": "object"}
  }
}
```

## Logging

-{"id":"ntm-161","title":"Implement ntm mail inbox command","description":"# Implement ntm mail inbox Command

## Purpose

Display an AGGREGATE inbox view showing messages across ALL agents in the project, not just NTM's coordinator messages.

## IMPORTANT: Aggregate View

This is not just NTM's inbox. It shows:
1. Messages TO any agent in the project (GreenCastle, BlueLake, etc.)
2. Messages FROM any agent in the project
3. Filtered by session agents if desired

This gives the user visibility into all agent-to-agent communication.

## Command Syntax

```bash
# Show all messages in project (across all agents)
ntm mail inbox myproject

# Filter to session's agents only
ntm mail inbox myproject --session-agents

# Filter to specific agent
ntm mail inbox myproject --agent GreenCastle

# Urgent only
ntm mail inbox myproject --urgent

# JSON for scripts
ntm mail inbox myproject --json
```

## Output Format

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Project Inbox: myproject (5 agents)                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ● #42 [URGENT] API Review Needed                            │
│   GreenCastle → BlueLake                                    │
│   2 minutes ago │ Thread: FEAT-123 │ ⚠️ Ack required         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ○ #41 Tests passing, ready for merge                        │
│   RedStone → GreenCastle, BlueLake                          │
│   15 minutes ago │ Thread: FEAT-123                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ○ #40 Starting work on src/api/**                           │
│   ntm_myproject → all                                       │
│   30 minutes ago │ (coordinator announcement)               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Showing messages involving: GreenCastle, BlueLake, RedStone, ntm_myproject
```

## Implementation

1. Discover all agents in project via Agent Mail
2. Fetch inbox for EACH agent
3. Deduplicate (same message may appear in multiple inboxes)
4. Sort by timestamp
5. Display with sender → recipient format

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Shows messages across ALL project agents
- [ ] Deduplication (message shown once even if multiple recipients)
- [ ] Filter by --session-agents, --agent, --urgent
- [ ] Clear indication of sender → recipient
- [ ] Coordinator messages distinguished from agent messages
- [ ] --json output for scripts","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ChartreuseLake","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:48.350455-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T20:42:27.003676-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T20:42:27.003676-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-161","depends_on_id":"ntm-yxa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:55.9288-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-5qzm","title":"Parse Claude /usage and /status output","description":"# Claude Usage/Status Parser

Implement regex-based parsing of Claude Code's /usage and /status output.

## Reference: CodexBar
From CodexBar documentation:
- Runs `claude /usage` and `/status` in local PTY
- Parses session/week/Sonnet percentages
- Extracts reset strings and account email/org/login method
- All parsing occurs on-device

## Expected Output Formats (research required)
Need to capture actual outputs to create accurate parsers. Expected patterns:

### /usage output
```
Session usage: 45% (estimated)
Weekly usage: 72%
Sonnet weekly: 38%
Resets: Monday 00:00 UTC
```

### /status output
```
Logged in as: user@example.com
Organization: Personal
Login method: Google OAuth
```

## Implementation
```go
// internal/quota/claude.go
package quota

type ClaudeQuota struct {
    SessionUsage  float64
    WeeklyUsage   float64
    SonnetUsage   float64
    ResetTime     string
    AccountEmail  string
    Organization  string
    LoginMethod   string
}

var claudeUsagePatterns = map[string]*regexp.Regexp{
    "session": regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)session[^:]*:\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%`),
    "weekly":  regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)weekly[^:]*:\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%`),
    "sonnet":  regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)sonnet[^:]*:\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%`),
    "reset":   regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)reset[^:]*:\s*(.+)`),
}

var claudeStatusPatterns = map[string]*regexp.Regexp{
    "email":   regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)logged\s+in\s+as:\s*(\S+)`),
    "org":     regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)organization:\s*(.+)`),
    "method":  regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)login\s+method:\s*(.+)`),
}

func ParseClaudeUsage(output string) (*ClaudeQuota, error) {
    // ... implementation
}

func ParseClaudeStatus(output string) (*ClaudeQuota, error) {
    // ... implementation
}
```

## Testing Strategy
1. Capture real /usage and /status outputs
2. Create test fixtures with various formats
3. Test edge cases (partial output, errors, etc.)
4. Test with empty/malformed output","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:14:00.304435-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T15:34:30.89684-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T15:34:30.89684-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-5qzm","depends_on_id":"ntm-p50g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:18:04.2629-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
-{"id":"ntm-5ujo","title":"Implement ntm changes and ntm conflicts commands","description":"# Implement ntm changes and ntm conflicts Commands

## Purpose

Display file changes attributed to agents and highlight conflicts.

## Commands

### ntm changes
```bash
ntm changes myproject
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Recent File Changes                                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ src/api/users.py        cc_1, cc_2      ⚠️ conflict         │
│ src/api/auth.py         cc_1                                │
│ tests/test_users.py     cod_1, cod_2                        │
│ docs/api.md             cod_2                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# With details
ntm changes myproject --details
```

### ntm conflicts
```bash
ntm conflicts myproject
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Conflicts Detected                                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ src/api/users.py                                            │
│   cc_1: +25 -10 lines (10 min ago)                         │
│   cc_2: +18 -8 lines (8 min ago)                           │
│                                                             │
│   Resolution options:                                       │
│   1. Keep cc_1's version: git checkout cc_1 -- src/api/... │
│   2. Keep cc_2's version: git checkout cc_2 -- src/api/... │
│   3. Manual merge needed                                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### ntm diff (optional enhancement)
```bash
ntm diff myproject cc_1 cc_2 src/api/users.py
# Shows diff between the two agents' versions
```

## JSON Output

```bash
ntm changes myproject --json
ntm conflicts myproject --json
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ntm changes shows file attribution
- [ ] ntm conflicts highlights multi-agent changes
- [ ] Line count changes shown
- [ ] Resolution suggestions provided
- [ ] JSON output for scripts","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:29:14.552083-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T20:24:36.03919-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-5ujo","depends_on_id":"ntm-t0ch","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:29:37.812663-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-5zc","title":"EPIC: Multi-Model Support","description":"# Multi-Model Support

## Background & Motivation

Currently, NTM treats agent types as monolithic:
- `--cc` spawns Claude with a fixed command
- `--cod` spawns Codex with a fixed command
- `--gmi` spawns Gemini with a fixed command

But in practice, power users need model flexibility:
- Use Claude Opus for architecture decisions (expensive, powerful)
- Use Claude Sonnet for implementation (cheaper, fast)
- Use GPT-4 for one task, o1 for reasoning-heavy tasks
- Mix Gemini Pro and Flash based on complexity

## The Problem

Real-world multi-agent workflows look like:
```
Architect (Opus) → Designer (Sonnet) → Implementer (Codex) → Reviewer (Opus)
```

NTM should enable this naturally without manual agent configuration.

## Proposed Syntax

### Spawn with model specifiers
```bash
# Basic: 2 Opus, 1 Sonnet
ntm spawn myproject --cc=2:opus --cc=1:sonnet

# Full model names
ntm spawn myproject --cc=1:claude-opus-4 --cc=2:claude-sonnet-4

# Codex variants
ntm spawn myproject --cod=1:gpt4 --cod=1:o1

# Gemini variants
ntm spawn myproject --gmi=1:pro --gmi=1:flash
```

### Add with model specifiers
```bash
ntm add myproject --cc=1:haiku  # Add cheap agent for simple tasks
```

## Model Aliases

Short aliases mapped to full model names in config:
```toml
[models.claude]
opus = "claude-opus-4"
sonnet = "claude-sonnet-4"
haiku = "claude-haiku"

[models.codex]
gpt4 = "gpt-4"
o1 = "o1"

[models.gemini]
pro = "gemini-pro"
flash = "gemini-flash"
```

## Pane Naming Enhancement

Current: `myproject__cc_1`
Enhanced: `myproject__cc_opus_1` or `myproject__cc_1_opus`

This enables filtering by model:
```bash
ntm send myproject --cc:opus "Architecture review needed"
ntm send myproject --cc:sonnet "Implement the design"
```

## Dashboard Display

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Panes                                            │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ● cc_1   Claude Opus    working                  │
│ ● cc_2   Claude Sonnet  idle                     │
│ ● cod_1  GPT-4          working                  │
│ ● cod_2  o1             thinking                 │
│ ● gmi_1  Gemini Pro     idle                     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] spawn accepts model specifiers (--cc=N:model)
- [ ] Model aliases configurable in config.toml
- [ ] Pane titles include model for identification
- [ ] send command can filter by model
- [ ] Status and dashboard show model info
- [ ] Robot mode includes model in agent output

## Implementation Notes

- Backwards compatible: `--cc=2` uses default model
- Model specifier parsed: count:model format
- Agent command template expanded with model variable","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:20:35.225729-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T16:17:37.840476-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T16:17:37.840476-05:00"}
 {"id":"ntm-5zs","title":"Create SSH wrapper for tmux commands","description":"Enable tmux commands to execute on remote hosts via SSH.

## What This Task Does

Create an abstraction layer that allows all tmux operations to run
either locally or on a remote host via SSH.

## Interface

$ ntm spawn myproject --ssh devbox --cc=4
$ ntm list --ssh devbox
$ ntm attach myproject --ssh devbox

## Technical Approach

Modify internal/tmux package to support remote execution:

type TmuxClient struct {
    Remote string // empty for local, "user@host" for remote
}

func (c *TmuxClient) Run(args ...string) (string, error) {
    if c.Remote == "" {
        return exec.Command("tmux", args...).Output()
    }
    sshArgs := append([]string{c.Remote, "tmux"}, args...)
    return exec.Command("ssh", sshArgs...).Output()
}

## SSH Configuration

Support SSH config hosts:
- $ ntm list --ssh devbox   # Uses ~/.ssh/config host
- $ ntm list --ssh user@192.168.1.100

Respect SSH options:
- ControlMaster for connection reuse
- Identity files
- Proxy jumps

## Error Handling

Connection failures:
- Clear error message with SSH exit code
- Suggest checking SSH connectivity
- Timeout handling

Auth failures:
- Detect auth errors
- Suggest ssh-add for key issues

## Session Context

Store remote host in tmux environment variable:
$ tmux set-environment -t myproject NTM_REMOTE_HOST devbox

When operating on existing session, detect remote host:
- If --ssh provided, use it
- Else check NTM_REMOTE_HOST
- Else assume local

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] TmuxClient abstraction supports local and remote
- [ ] All existing tmux functions work with remote
- [ ] SSH config hosts supported
- [ ] Connection errors handled gracefully
- [ ] Remote host stored with session

 {"id":"ntm-lla","title":"Implement session state save/restore","description":"Save and restore session state for disaster recovery.

## Rationale
If I'm orchestrating 8 agents on a complex task and:
- System crashes
- Network disconnects
- Need to pause and resume later

I need to be able to save the current state and restore it.

## Save Operation
```bash
ntm --robot-save myproject --output=state.json
ntm --robot-save myproject  # auto-saves to ~/.local/share/ntm/saves/
```

## State Includes
```json
{
  "saved_at": "ISO8601",
  "session": "myproject",
  "working_dir": "/path/to/project",
  "agents": [
    {
      "pane": "0.1",
      "type": "claude",
      "command": "claude",
      "recent_context": ["last 50 lines..."]
    }
  ],
  "layout": "tiled",
  "active_pane": "0.1",
  "command_history": [...],
  "beads_state": {...}  // if beads integration available
}
```

## Restore Operation
```bash
ntm --robot-restore state.json
ntm --robot-restore state.json --dry-run  # show what would happen
```

## Restore Process
1. Create session with same name
2. Create panes with correct layout
3. Start agents in each pane
4. Optionally replay context (send last N lines as context)

## Limitations
- Cannot restore exact agent state (no agent-side state)
- Context replay is best-effort
- External state (files changed) not tracked","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"BlueLake","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:59:00.354106-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T13:18:21.607962-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T13:18:21.607962-05:00"}

-{"id":"ntm-t0ch","title":"Implement file conflict detection","description":"# Implement File Conflict Detection

## Purpose

Detect when multiple agents modify the same file, potentially creating conflicts.

## Detection Logic

```go
type Conflict struct {
    Path   string
    Agents []AgentChange
}

func DetectConflicts(changes []AgentChange) []Conflict {
    byPath := make(map[string][]AgentChange)
    for _, c := range changes {
        byPath[c.File] = append(byPath[c.File], c)
    }
    
    var conflicts []Conflict
    for path, agents := range byPath {
        if len(agents) > 1 {
            conflicts = append(conflicts, Conflict{
                Path:   path,
                Agents: agents,
            })
        }
    }
    return conflicts
}
```

## Severity Levels

- **Warning**: Same file modified by 2+ agents (may or may not conflict)
- **Critical**: Same lines modified (if we can detect via git diff)

## Integration with ntm send --all

After broadcast completes:
```
ntm send myproject --all "Implement the feature"
# ... agents work ...
# On completion:
⚠️ Potential conflict: src/api/users.py modified by cc_1, cc_2
  Run `ntm conflicts myproject` for details
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Detect same-file modifications across agents
- [ ] Warn user after broadcasts
- [ ] ntm conflicts command shows details
- [ ] Severity classification (warning vs critical)","notes":"Picking up highest-priority free task; conflict detection","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:29:14.387332-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T20:43:54.633041-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T20:43:54.633041-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-t0ch","depends_on_id":"ntm-xkvu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:29:37.60962-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-t7ol","title":"Phase 3: Auto-Trigger on Rate Limit Detection","description":"# Phase 3: Auto-Trigger on Rate Limit Detection

## What This Is
Automatically trigger rotation assistance when a rate limit is detected, instead of requiring user to manually run `ntm rotate`.

## Leverages Existing Infrastructure
NTM already has rate limit detection in `internal/health/health.go`:
```go
var errorPatterns = []struct {
    Pattern *regexp.Regexp
    Type    string
    Message string
}{
    {regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)rate.?limit`), "rate_limit", "Rate limit detected"},
    {regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)429`), "rate_limit", "HTTP 429 rate limit"},
    {regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)too.?many.?requests`), "rate_limit", "Too many requests"},
    {regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)quota.?exceeded`), "rate_limit", "Quota exceeded"},
}
```

And notification in `internal/notify/notify.go`:
```go
EventRateLimit EventType = "agent.rate_limit"
```

We just need to **connect** limit detection → rotation trigger.

## Implementation Approach

### Option A: Background Monitor (Recommended)
Add rotation trigger to the resilience monitor:

```go
// In internal/resilience/monitor.go

func (m *Monitor) handleRateLimit(agent *AgentState, waitSeconds int) {
    // Existing notification
    if m.cfg.RateLimit.Notify {
        m.notifier.Notify(notify.NewRateLimitEvent(...))
    }
    
    // NEW: Trigger rotation assistance
    if m.cfg.Rotation.AutoTrigger {
        m.triggerRotationAssistance(agent)
    }
}

func (m *Monitor) triggerRotationAssistance(agent *AgentState) {
    // Send desktop notification with action hint
    m.notifier.Notify(notify.Event{
        Type:    notify.EventRotationNeeded,
        Message: fmt.Sprintf("Rate limit hit! Run: ntm rotate %s --pane=%d", m.session, agent.PaneIndex),
    })
    
    // Optionally auto-initiate rotation (if configured)
    if m.cfg.Rotation.AutoInitiate {
        // Start the rotation flow in a separate goroutine
        go m.initiateRotation(agent)
    }
}
```

### Option B: Dashboard Integration
When dashboard is open and detects limit, show prominent alert:

```
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  ⚠️  RATE LIMIT DETECTED on pane 0 (Claude)                   ║
║                                                               ║
║  Press 'r' to rotate account, or run: ntm rotate --pane=0     ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

### Option C: tmux Alert (Simplest)
When running `ntm spawn` with monitoring, show tmux message:

```go
func alertRateLimit(session string, paneIdx int) {
    msg := fmt.Sprintf("Rate limit! Run: ntm rotate %s --pane=%d", session, paneIdx)
    exec.Command("tmux", "display-message", "-d", "10000", msg).Run()
}
```

## Configuration
```toml
[rotation]
# Existing fields...
auto_trigger = true       # Show notification when limit detected
auto_initiate = false     # Actually start rotation automatically (aggressive)
```

## What This Builds On
- Phase 1: Core rotation command
- Existing health detection
- Existing notification system

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Desktop notification when rate limit detected
- [ ] Notification includes command to run
- [ ] Dashboard shows alert (if open)
- [ ] Optional auto-initiation (disabled by default)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:14:52.846787-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T17:39:00.21314-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T17:39:00.21314-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-t7ol","depends_on_id":"ntm-cs4z","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:18:01.91758-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-ws2","title":"Create internal/output package with JSON/text formatters","description":"Create a unified output formatting system that all commands will use.

## What This Task Does

Create a new internal/output package that provides:
1. OutputFormat type (JSON, Text, Table)
2. Formatter interface that commands implement
3. Root-level --json flag handling
4. Consistent JSON schema patterns

## Why This Is First

This is the foundation for all JSON output. Every command will use this
package, so it needs to be designed well upfront.

## Technical Details

Package structure:
internal/output/
├── format.go      # OutputFormat enum, Formatter interface
├── json.go        # JSON-specific formatting helpers
├── text.go        # Text-specific formatting (existing patterns)
└── schema.go      # Common JSON schema types

Key types:
type OutputFormat int
const (
    FormatText OutputFormat = iota
    FormatJSON
)

type Formatter interface {
    Format(data interface{}, format OutputFormat, w io.Writer) error
}

Common JSON patterns:
- Timestamps as ISO 8601 strings
- Errors include 'error' field with message
- Lists are always arrays (even empty)
- Nested objects for complex data

## Implementation Steps

1. Create internal/output directory
2. Define OutputFormat enum and detection logic
3. Create Formatter interface
4. Add JSON helper functions (marshal with indent, error wrapping)
5. Add root --json flag to cobra root command
6. Create context passing for format flag

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] internal/output package exists with clean API
- [ ] --json flag added to root command
- [ ] Format detection works (flag, env var, pipe detection)
- [ ] JSON output is consistently formatted
- [ ] Package is documented with examples

## Current State
- Coverage: 5.4%
- Tests: 4 existing (session name validation, agent type detection)

## Test Approach
Use REAL tmux sessions. Each test creates a unique session and cleans up after.

## Test Cases

### session.go

#### Session Management
- [ ] TestCreateSession - creates real session, verifies it exists
- [ ] TestCreateSessionWithDir - verifies working directory is set
- [ ] TestCreateSessionDuplicate - error handling for existing session
- [ ] TestKillSession - kills session, verifies gone
- [ ] TestSessionExists - true/false for existing/non-existing

#### Pane Operations
- [ ] TestSplitWindow - creates new pane, returns correct ID
- [ ] TestSplitWindowMultiple - creates 5 panes, verifies indices
- [ ] TestSetPaneTitle - sets title, verifies with list-panes
- [ ] TestGetPanes - lists all panes with correct metadata
- [ ] TestCapturePaneOutput - captures recent output

#### Layout Operations
- [ ] TestApplyTiledLayout - tiles panes, verifies layout
- [ ] TestSelectPane - selects specific pane
- [ ] TestZoomPane - zooms and unzooms

#### Key Sending
- [ ] TestSendKeys - sends text, verifies in pane output
- [ ] TestSendKeysWithEnter - sends text with enter key
- [ ] TestSendInterrupt - sends Ctrl+C

#### Agent Type Detection
- [x] TestAgentTypeFromTitle - existing test, expand coverage
- [ ] TestAgentTypeFromTitle_EdgeCases - empty, special chars

#### Attachment
- [ ] TestAttachSession - attach to session (difficult to test)
- [ ] TestSwitchToSession - switch client to session

### Helper Functions
- [ ] TestInTmux - detect if running in tmux
- [ ] TestIsInstalled - detect tmux installation

## Test Helpers Needed
```go
func createTestSession(t *testing.T) string {
    name := fmt.Sprintf("ntm_test_%d", time.Now().UnixNano())
    t.Cleanup(func() { KillSession(name) })
    CreateSession(name, os.TempDir())
    return name
}
```

## Skip Conditions

- Skip attach tests if not in tmux","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:20:02.989397-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:55.911298-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:55.911298-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-x0h","depends_on_id":"ntm-idx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:22:33.599967-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

-{"id":"ntm-xkvu","title":"Implement file modification tracking per agent","description":"# Implement File Modification Tracking Per Agent

## Purpose

Track which files each agent modifies, enabling conflict detection and contribution attribution.

## Approach

### Method 1: File Mtime Tracking
```go
type FileSnapshot struct {
    Path  string
    Mtime time.Time
    Size  int64
}

func SnapshotDirectory(dir string) map[string]FileSnapshot {
    // Walk directory, record mtime/size for each file
}

func DetectChanges(before, after map[string]FileSnapshot) []FileChange {
    // Compare snapshots, return changed files
}
```

### Method 2: Git Status Parsing
```go
func GetUncommittedChanges() ([]string, error) {
    // git status --porcelain
    // Parse output for modified/added/deleted files
}
```

### Attribution to Agents
Track changes with timestamps:
```go
type AgentChange struct {
    Agent     string    // Pane identifier
    File      string
    Timestamp time.Time
    ChangeType string  // modified, added, deleted
}
```

When `ntm send` completes, snapshot files again and attribute changes to the targeted agents.

## Limitations

- Can't perfectly attribute if multiple agents running simultaneously
- Best-effort: attribute to agent(s) that were sent prompts most recently
- Works best with sequential workflows

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Snapshot file state before prompts
- [ ] Detect changes after prompts complete
- [ ] Attribute changes to agents (best-effort)
- [ ] Store change history for comparison
- [ ] Handle git-ignored files appropriately","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:29:14.145569-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T20:43:37.438383-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T20:43:37.438383-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-xle","title":"Implement cross-platform clipboard detection and access","description":"Create internal/clipboard package for cross-platform clipboard access.

## What This Task Does

Create a clipboard abstraction that works on macOS, Linux (X11/Wayland),
and WSL. This provides the foundation for clipboard-related features.

## Platform Detection

Detect platform and available clipboard tools:

macOS:
- pbcopy / pbpaste (always available)

Linux X11:
- xclip -selection clipboard
- xsel --clipboard

Linux Wayland:
- wl-copy / wl-paste

WSL:
- clip.exe (write only)
- powershell.exe Get-Clipboard (read)

Fallback:
- tmux's internal clipboard (with warning about limitations)

## Implementation

package clipboard

type Clipboard interface {
    Copy(content string) error
    Paste() (string, error)
    Available() bool
}

func New() (Clipboard, error) {
    switch runtime.GOOS {
    case "darwin":
        return &macOSClipboard{}, nil
    case "linux":
        if isWSL() {
            return &wslClipboard{}, nil
        }
        if isWayland() {
            return &waylandClipboard{}, nil
        }
        return &x11Clipboard{}, nil
    }
    return &tmuxClipboard{}, nil
}

## Error Handling

- Tool not found: Clear error with installation suggestion
- Permission denied: Suggest running with appropriate access
- Empty clipboard: Return empty string, not error

## Testing

Need to handle:
- CI environments (no display, no clipboard)
- SSH sessions (no local clipboard)
- Container environments

Skip clipboard tests or mock in these environments.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Clipboard works on macOS
- [ ] Clipboard works on Linux X11
- [ ] Clipboard works on Linux Wayland
- [ ] Clipboard works on WSL
- [ ] Graceful fallback when tools missing
